claim: Transformers outperform RNNs on long-sequence modeling tasks.
Larger language models exhibit emergent abilities not present in smaller models.
Reinforcement learning from human feedback improves the helpfulness of language models without degrading their factual accuracy.
Retrieval-augmented generation reduces hallucination in large language models.
Chain-of-thought prompting improves reasoning performance in large language models.
Mixture-of-experts architectures achieve better compute efficiency than dense models at equivalent performance.
Federated learning preserves model accuracy compared to centralized training on the same data.
Graph neural networks outperform traditional methods on node classification tasks.
Adversarial training improves model robustness without significantly hurting clean accuracy.
Knowledge distillation allows small models to match the performance of much larger teacher models.
Self-supervised pretraining improves downstream performance on low-resource NLP tasks.
Diffusion models produce higher-quality images than GANs.
Post-training quantization to 4-bit precision preserves most of the accuracy of full-precision LLMs.
Few-shot prompting outperforms zero-shot prompting on most NLP benchmarks.
Multi-agent LLM debate improves factual accuracy over single-agent responses.
Vision transformers outperform convolutional neural networks on image classification when given sufficient training data.
Continual learning methods effectively mitigate catastrophic forgetting in neural networks.
Differentially private training significantly degrades model utility compared to non-private training.
Sparse attention mechanisms achieve comparable performance to full attention at lower computational cost.
In-context learning ability emerges primarily as a function of model scale rather than training data composition.
